mappings.rationaleAI: Covers Singapore's separation from Malaysia (1965) and subsequent development under Lee Kuan Yew's People's Action Party, including rapid economic growth, authoritarian governance, and Singapore's role as a global financial and trade hub. Directly addresses B.2.c on the PAP regime and B.2 on Malaya/Singapore since 1920.

Covers Bangladesh's emergence as an independent nation in 1971 following the liberation war, the government of Mujibur Rahman, subsequent alternations between parliamentary democracy and military dictatorship, and later administrations — directly mapping to A.4.

Covers Burma's British colonial period, emergence of Burmese nationalism, Japanese occupation (1942–45), postwar independence under U Nu, and decades of military government under Ne Win and successors — directly addressing B.1.a and B.1.b.

Covers Pakistan's establishment in 1947 and its alternating civilian and military governments including Jinnah, Ayub Khan, Bhutto, Zia, and Musharraf, as well as the 1971 secession of Bangladesh — directly addressing A.3.a and A.3.b.

Covers French Indochina, Ho Chi Minh's resistance, the First Indochina War, Geneva Accords, the Vietnam War division between North and South, US intervention, reunification in 1976, and subsequent Doi Moi reforms — spanning the full range of B.4 sub-paths.

Gandhi is the central figure of A.1.a, covering his satyagraha movement of nonviolent resistance, mass mobilization campaigns including the Salt March and Non-Cooperation movement, and the push for Indian independence from British rule up to 1947.

Covers British Malaya's colonial economy, Japanese occupation, independence in 1957, the formation and expansion of the Malaysian federation, communal tensions, the New Economic Policy, and Mahathir's administration — directly addressing B.2.a and B.2.b.

Covers the constitutional monarchy established in 1932, militaristic nationalism and Japanese occupation during WWII, postwar political instability, and recurring cycles of military domination and civilian government — directly mapping to B.3.a and B.3.b.

Covers the full arc from Gandhi's independence movement and the Government of India Act (1935) through partition (1947) to Nehru's parliamentary system, state reorganization, foreign policy nonalignment, the Kashmir dispute, and the 1962 border conflict with China — addressing A.1 and A.2 comprehensively.

Covers Dutch colonial administration, nationalist movements, Japanese occupation, Sukarno's proclamation of independence (1945), Dutch attempts to regain control, formal independence (1949), Guided Democracy, the 1965 coup, Suharto's long rule, and democratization from 1998 — directly addressing all C.1 sub-paths.

Covers U.S. administration in the 1920s–30s, the growth of nationalist parties, commonwealth status (1935), Japanese occupation (1941–45), and the return to U.S. control — directly addressing C.2 and C.2.a.

Mao's government directed China's invasion of Tibet (1950) and assertion of complete control after 1959, covered in A.6, and his leadership underlies the 1962 Sino-Indian border conflict referenced in A.2.b's treatment of Nehru's foreign policy.

WWII's Pacific theatre was pivotal across this section: Japanese occupation of Burma (B.1.a), Malaya and Singapore (B.2.a), Indonesia (C.1.a), and the Philippines (C.2.a) all reshaped the path to decolonization and independence, and wartime events also affected the Indian independence movement (A.1.b).

The British Empire is the colonial authority against which India, Burma, Malaya, Singapore, and Ceylon all sought independence — covering the Government of India Act, constitutional reforms, and the transition to dominion status and independence across A.1, A.5, B.1, and B.2.a.

The Cold War provides the geopolitical context for Nehru's nonalignment policy (A.2.b), the communist insurgency in Malaya (B.2.a), and the Vietnam War's North-South division, U.S. military intervention, and ultimate communist victory (B.4.c–d).

Japan's wartime occupation of Burma, Malaya, Singapore, Thailand, Indonesia, and the Philippines is central to B.1.a, B.2.a, B.3.a, C.1.a, and C.2.a, and the effects of WWII on the Indian independence movement are addressed in A.1.b.
China's invasion of Tibet (1950) and assertion of complete control (1959) directly addresses A.6, and the 1962 Sino-Indian border incursion is a key element of Nehru's foreign policy in A.2.b.
